Name: Damian Lillard
Fun Fact: He hit one of the most iconic buzzer-beaters in playoff history against OKC in 2019 - pointed at his wrist as if checking the time - then walked off

Name: Jim Otto
Fun Fact: He wore #00 for every one of his 210 consecutive starts - a number the NFL doesn't even officially allow anymore - and made the Hall of Fame doing it
Notes: Number 00 is distinct from 0. Otto defines this number in football as Parish does in basketball.

Name: Robert Parish
Stat Label: Regular Season Games (all-time record at retirement)
Fun Fact: He played 1611 regular season games - more than anyone in NBA history at the time - and won three championships in Boston alongside Bird and McHale. They called him The Chief and he earned every syllable.
Notes: Number 00 is distinct from 0. Parish defines this number.

Name: Russell Westbrook
Fun Fact: He broke Oscar Robertson's 55-year-old triple-double record and didn't stop - he now has nearly 200, a number so absurd the stat feels made up

Name: Oscar Robertson
Fun Fact: He averaged a triple-double for an entire season in 1961-62 - a feat so absurd it took 55 years for anyone to do it again

Name: Terry Sawchuk
Fun Fact: He recorded 103 shutouts - a record that still stands today - and played through injuries that would have ended anyone else's career

Name: Victor Wembanyama
Fun Fact: He's 7-foot-4 with guard skills and a 8-foot wingspan - NBA coaches said they had never seen anything like him before he played a single game

Name: Warren Moon
Fun Fact: He was passed over by every NFL team in 1978 because of his race - spent 6 years dominating the CFL before the NFL finally came calling

Name: Brian Leetch
Fun Fact: He won the Conn Smythe in 1994 as a defenseman - only the third D-man ever to win playoff MVP - and ended the Rangers' 54-year Cup drought

Name: Derek Jeter
Fun Fact: He made The Flip - a relay throw nobody should have been near - to nail Jeremy Giambi at the plate and save a playoff series

Name: Allen Iverson
Fun Fact: He was 6 feet tall and 165 pounds and led the NBA in scoring four times - the Answer proved size didn't matter if your heart was big enough

Name: Babe Ruth
Fun Fact: He was such a dominant pitcher that the Yankees converted him to an outfielder just so he could hit every day - and he became the greatest power hitter who ever lived

Name: Dwyane Wade
Fun Fact: In the 2006 Finals he averaged 34.7 points and single-handedly willed the Heat back from 2-0 down to win the championship

Name: Diana Taurasi
Fun Fact: Widely considered the greatest player in WNBA history, she led Phoenix to three championships and became the league's all-time scoring leader

Name: Brett Favre
Fun Fact: He started 297 consecutive games at quarterback - the ultimate ironman streak in the NFL - while winning three straight MVP awards in the 1990s

Name: Bobby Orr
Fun Fact: He flew through the air with his arms out after scoring the 1970 Cup winner - the most iconic image in hockey history

Name: Lou Gehrig
Fun Fact: He played 2,130 consecutive games - and when ALS finally forced him to stop he stood at home plate and called himself the luckiest man on the face of the earth

Name: Anthony Edwards
Fun Fact: He plays every game like he's having the time of his life - and he might already be the second-best player in the NBA

Name: Joe DiMaggio
Fun Fact: He hit in 56 consecutive games in 1941 - a record so untouchable that statisticians say it's the most unlikely sports achievement in history

Name: Nicklas Lidstrom
Fun Fact: He won seven Norris Trophies as the best defenseman in hockey - a standard of excellence over 20 years that may never be matched

Name: Franz Beckenbauer
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'Der Kaiser', he revolutionized the libero position and captained West Germany to the 1974 World Cup

Name: Bill Russell
Fun Fact: The NBA retired his number across all 30 teams in 2022 - 55 years after his last championship - because some legacies take time to fully understand

Name: Mickey Mantle
Fun Fact: He hit the longest measured home run in history - 565 feet - at 21 years old - and did it on knees that should have ended his career a decade earlier

Name: John Elway
Fun Fact: He led 'The Drive' in the 1987 AFC Championship Game - a 98-yard march in the final minutes that became one of the most famous drives in NFL history
Notes: One of the most iconic quarterbacks of the late 20th century

Name: Phil Esposito
Fun Fact: He was the first player in NHL history to score 100 points in a season - in 1968-69 - and he did it so comfortably that everyone knew the game had changed

Name: Cristiano Ronaldo
Fun Fact: His iconic 'CR7' brand is built around the number he wore at Manchester United, Real Madrid, and with Portugal

Name: David Beckham
Fun Fact: His last-minute free kick against Greece in 2001 sent England to the World Cup and became one of the most famous goals in English football history

Name: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander
Fun Fact: He became the most efficient elite scorer in the NBA - averaging 30 points on 53% shooting - the quietest superstar in the league

Name: Alex Ovechkin
Fun Fact: He passed Wayne Gretzky's all-time goals record in 2024 - the record everyone said was untouchable - and celebrated like he'd been waiting his whole life

Name: Cal Ripken Jr.
Fun Fact: He played 2,632 consecutive games - breaking Gehrig's record that everyone said was untouchable - and did it as a shortstop, not a DH

Name: Cale Makar
Fun Fact: He won the Norris, Conn Smythe, and Stanley Cup in the same season at age 23 - one of the greatest seasons a defenseman has ever played

Name: Kobe Bryant
Fun Fact: He wore both #8 and #24 - both retired by the Lakers - the only player in NBA history with two retired numbers at the same franchise

Name: Erling Haaland
Stat Label: Premier League Goals in a Season
Fun Fact: He scored 36 Premier League goals in his first season in England - breaking the all-time record by a massive margin

Name: Mia Hamm
Fun Fact: She became the youngest player ever called up to the US Women's National Team at age 15 and helped lead the U.S. to two World Cup titles

Name: Gordie Howe
Fun Fact: The Gordie Howe Hat Trick is a goal, an assist, and a fight in one game - named for him because he did it so often it became its own category

Name: Maurice Richard
Fun Fact: When the NHL suspended him in 1955, Montreal fans rioted in the streets - the Richard Riot showed what he meant to an entire people

Name: Ronaldo Nazário
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'R9', he returned from devastating knee injuries to lead Brazil to the 2002 World Cup and win the Golden Boot

Name: Roger Maris
Fun Fact: Broke Babe Ruth’s single-season home run record with 61 in 1961 during one of baseball’s most dramatic seasons

Name: Ted Williams
Fun Fact: He hit .406 in 1941 and nobody has done it since - and he lost five prime years to two military tours and came back and was still the best hitter alive

Name: Auston Matthews
Fun Fact: He scored 69 goals in 2023-24 - the most in the NHL since the 1990s - and did it with a shot so fast goalies can barely react

Name: Marta
Fun Fact: She has won FIFA World Player of the Year six times - more than any player male or female - and carried Brazil to global prominence

Name: Guy Lafleur
Fun Fact: He used to drive to the rink before dawn just to skate alone - that obsession drove five consecutive Stanley Cups

Name: Pelé
Fun Fact: He won three World Cups - at 17, 22, and 29 - across three different decades - and scored over 1,000 career goals in professional football

Name: Diego Maradona
Fun Fact: In the 1986 World Cup quarterfinal he scored both the 'Hand of God' goal and the 'Goal of the Century' against England within four minutes

Name: Lionel Messi
Fun Fact: He finally won the 2022 World Cup with Argentina, cementing his place in the debate as the greatest footballer of all time

Name: Zinedine Zidane
Fun Fact: He scored two headers in the 1998 World Cup final to give France its first World Cup title

Name: Ronaldinho
Fun Fact: He once received a standing ovation from Real Madrid fans at the Santiago Bernabéu after scoring two incredible goals for Barcelona

Name: Steve Nash
Fun Fact: He won back-to-back MVPs without a single All-NBA defensive team - proving a point guard could win the award on pure offensive brilliance alone

Name: Isiah Thomas
Fun Fact: In the 1988 Finals he scored 25 points in one quarter on a badly sprained ankle - hobbling up and down the court - one of the great performances in Finals history

Name: Mark Messier
Fun Fact: He guaranteed a win in Game 6 of the 1994 ECF when the Rangers were down 3-2 - and then scored a hat trick - the greatest guarantee in hockey history

Name: Mohamed Salah
Fun Fact: He scored 32 Premier League goals in 2017-18 - breaking the record for a 38-game season - and has been Liverpool's best player for seven straight years

Name: Tom Brady
Fun Fact: He was the 199th pick in the 2000 NFL Draft and went on to win more Super Bowls than any franchise in league history
Notes: Widely considered the greatest quarterback in NFL history

Name: Joe Namath
Fun Fact: He guaranteed Super Bowl III victory as a 17-point underdog - and then won it - the most famous guarantee in sports history and the moment the AFL became real

Name: John Stockton
Fun Fact: He has 5,000 more career assists than the second-best passer in NBA history - a gap so large it may never be closed

Name: Christine Sinclair
Fun Fact: She scored more international goals than any player in soccer history, male or female, and led Canada to Olympic gold in 2021

Name: Dan Marino
Fun Fact: He set the single-season passing record in 1984 with 5,084 yards - it stood for 27 years - and he never won a Super Bowl which makes him the greatest what-if in NFL history

Name: Wilt Chamberlain
Fun Fact: He scored 100 points in a single NBA game in 1962 - a record so far beyond anyone else that it seems fictional - and averaged 50 points per game that entire season

Name: Alex Rodriguez
Hometown: New York City, New York, USA
Fun Fact: He became the youngest player in MLB history to reach 500 home runs at age 32
Notes: Legacy complicated by PED suspension but statistical dominance is undeniable

Name: Brendan Shanahan
Fun Fact: He scored 656 goals and dropped the gloves more than almost anyone - a Hall of Famer who combined skill and toughness in a way nobody else did

Name: Pete Rose
Fun Fact: He has 4,256 career hits - 130 more than the second all-time - and he's not in the Hall of Fame because of gambling - the most complicated legacy in baseball
Notes: Gambling ban complicates but doesn't erase the stats

Name: Abby Wambach
Fun Fact: She scored one of the most famous goals in World Cup history with a last-minute header against Brazil in the 2011 quarterfinal

Name: Johan Cruyff
Fun Fact: His 'Cruyff Turn' move became one of the most famous skills in soccer history and symbolized the Total Football revolution

Name: Nikola Jokic
Fun Fact: He's a 6-foot-11 center who makes passes only point guards are supposed to make - three MVPs and a championship make him the most unique dominant player in NBA history
Notes: Active but passes Legend test - per tier list

Name: Thurman Munson
Fun Fact: He died in a plane crash in 1979 at 32 - the Yankees retired his number immediately - the most beloved Yankee of the 1970s dynasty

Name: Joe Montana
Fun Fact: He was 3-for-3 in Super Bowl situations that required a game-winning drive - never threw an interception in four Super Bowls - Cool Joe never flinched

Name: Reggie Jackson
Fun Fact: He hit three consecutive home runs on three consecutive pitches from three different pitchers in Game 6 of the 1977 World Series - the most theatrical performance in baseball history

Name: Shohei Ohtani
Fun Fact: He's the only player in the history of baseball to be an All-Star pitcher and a home run threat at the same time - Ruth is the only comparison and even that falls short

Name: Peyton Manning
Fun Fact: He set the single-season TD record with 55 in 2013 at age 37 - calling audibles at the line with surgical precision - the most cerebral QB who ever played

Name: Johnny Unitas
Fun Fact: He threw a touchdown pass in 47 consecutive games - a record that stood for 52 years - and did it while wearing high-top black cleats that became the symbol of his era

Name: Steve Yzerman
Fun Fact: He waited 11 years to win his first Stanley Cup as Detroit's captain - and when he finally won it he skated around the ice alone with the Cup at 2am

Name: Tony Gwynn
Fun Fact: In the strike-shortened 1994 season he was hitting .394 when the season stopped - the closest anyone came to .400 since Ted Williams in 1941

Name: Barry Sanders
Fun Fact: He retired at his peak with 15,269 rushing yards - 1,500 shy of the all-time record - because he didn't want to play for a bad team anymore - the most shocking retirement in NFL history

Name: Mike Schmidt
Fun Fact: He hit 548 home runs and won 10 Gold Gloves - the most complete third baseman in baseball history - and the Phillies' only World Series title came with him at third

Name: Roberto Clemente
Fun Fact: He died on New Year's Eve 1972 on a relief flight to earthquake victims in Nicaragua - the Hall of Fame waived the five-year waiting period and inducted him immediately

Name: Tim Duncan
Fun Fact: He won five championships spanning three different decades - doing it quietly and fundamentally while everyone else chased flash - the greatest power forward ever

Name: Emmitt Smith
Fun Fact: He is the all-time NFL rushing leader with 18,355 yards - and won three Super Bowls in the 1990s Cowboys dynasty while doing it

Name: Mike Bossy
Fun Fact: He scored 50 goals in 50 games in the 1980-81 season - matching Rocket Richard's legendary mark - and did it in his last game of that stretch with two goals in the final period

Name: Caitlin Clark
Fun Fact: Her deep-range shooting and playmaking at Iowa helped drive record TV audiences for women's college basketball and made her the NCAA's all-time leading scorer

Name: Michael Jordan
Fun Fact: The NBA retired his number across all 30 franchises - then granted one exception for LeBron James - the only player deemed equivalent enough to wear it
Notes: SACRED - LeBron is the documented exception

Name: LeBron James
Fun Fact: He's the all-time NBA scoring leader and the only player ever granted an exception to wear a league-retired number - wearing Jordan's sacred #23
Notes: The documented exception to the Sacred retirement

Name: Kobe Bryant
Fun Fact: He switched from #8 to #24 midcareer - a reset and rebirth - and both numbers were retired by the Lakers making him the only player in NBA history with two retired numbers at one franchise

Name: Maya Moore
Fun Fact: She stepped away from basketball at her peak to help overturn a wrongful conviction, later marrying the man she helped free

Name: Ken Griffey Jr.
Fun Fact: His smooth left-handed swing made him one of the most iconic players of the 1990s and a first-ballot Hall of Famer

Name: Rickey Henderson
Fun Fact: He stole 1,406 bases - 468 more than the second all-time - and referred to himself in the third person as the greatest of all time which, honestly, he was

Name: Ty Law
Fun Fact: He intercepted Kurt Warner for a pick-six in Super Bowl XXXVI, a play that helped launch the Patriots dynasty

Name: Barry Bonds
Fun Fact: He hit 73 home runs in 2001 - the single-season record - and holds the all-time HR record with 762 - the most complicated legacy in baseball alongside Pete Rose
Notes: PED era complicates but stats are undeniable

Name: Mark McGwire
Fun Fact: His 1998 home run chase with Sammy Sosa captivated the baseball world and revived the sport after the 1994 strike

Name: Vladimir Guerrero
Fun Fact: He swung at balls a foot outside the strike zone and hit them for extra bases - the most dangerous bad-ball hitter in baseball history

Name: Marie-Philip Poulin
Fun Fact: She scored the gold medal winning goal in the 2010, 2014, and 2022 Olympics - three different Olympics, three times Canada needed a goal, three times she delivered
Notes: ACTIVE - equal weight to MacKinnon per tier list

Name: Nathan MacKinnon
Fun Fact: He has won multiple Hart Trophies and a Stanley Cup - widely considered the best player in the NHL - the most complete forward in the game today
Notes: Active but passes Legend test per tier list

Name: Marie-Philip Poulin
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'Captain Clutch', she scored goals in three Olympic gold medal games for Canada

Name: Martin Brodeur
Fun Fact: He has 691 career wins - a record so far ahead of everyone else it's essentially untouchable - and won three Stanley Cups doing it

Name: Stephen Curry
Fun Fact: He single-handedly made the three-pointer the centerpiece of basketball offense - four championships and the unanimous MVP with the 73-win season
Notes: Active but passes Legend test per tier list

Name: Patrick Roy
Fun Fact: He used to talk to his goalposts before games and demand a trade mid-game from the Montreal bench in front of 20,000 people - the most magnetic personality in hockey history

Name: Magic Johnson
Fun Fact: He played center in the deciding game of his rookie Finals - a 6-foot-9 point guard starting at center and scoring 42 points - the performance that announced he was different

Name: Sandy Koufax
Fun Fact: From 1963-1966 he was the most dominant pitcher in baseball history - four no-hitters including a perfect game - then he retired at 30 because his arm gave out

Name: Larry Bird
Fun Fact: He once told the other team exactly where he was going to shoot from and then did it - three MVPs and three championships next door to Magic's 32

Name: Patrick Ewing
Fun Fact: He carried the Knicks on his back for 15 seasons and never won a title - the greatest player to never win a ring alongside Barkley and Stockton

Name: Jose Canseco
Fun Fact: In 1988 he became the first player in MLB history to record 40 home runs and 40 stolen bases in a single season

Name: Shaquille O'Neal
Fun Fact: He was so physically dominant in 2000-2002 that referees had to create new rules just to slow him down - three consecutive Finals MVPs

Name: Charles Barkley
Fun Fact: Despite being undersized for a power forward he dominated the NBA in the 1990s and won MVP in 1993 leading the Suns to the Finals

Name: David Ortiz
Fun Fact: One of the greatest postseason hitters ever, he led the Red Sox to three World Series titles and delivered one of the most famous grand slams in playoff history in the 2013 ALCS

Name: Giannis Antetokounmpo
Fun Fact: Delivered one of the greatest Finals performances ever with 50 points to clinch the Bucks’ first championship in 50 years

Name: Walter Payton
Fun Fact: Sweetness - he ran with the grace of a dancer and the force of a freight train - the most complete running back in NFL history

Name: Kevin Durant
Fun Fact: He named his number 35 in honor of his AAU coach Charles Craig, who was murdered at age 35 and helped shape Durant's career
Notes: One of the greatest pure scorers in NBA history

Name: Gale Sayers
Fun Fact: He was inducted into the Hall of Fame at 34 - the youngest ever - after injuries cut his career short - Brian's Song made him known to a generation beyond football

Name: Dirk Nowitzki
Fun Fact: He was the first European player to win NBA MVP and invented the fadeaway one-legged jumper that every big in the world now tries to copy

Name: Tom Seaver
Fun Fact: He was so good the Mets named him Tom Terrific and built a championship around him - three Cy Youngs and the 1969 Miracle Mets

Name: Jackie Robinson
Fun Fact: His number is retired across all of baseball - not one team, all of them - and every April 15th every player in baseball wears 42 because what he did was bigger than baseball

Name: Mariano Rivera
Fun Fact: His cut fastball was so dominant that hitters knew it was coming and still couldn't hit it - he became the first player ever unanimously elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Notes: Greatest closer in baseball history; #42 already appears as SACRED for Jackie Robinson but Rivera is the defining player of the number in the modern era

Name: Troy Polamalu
Fun Fact: He played with long flowing hair that flew behind him when he blitzed - a supernatural force at safety who seemed to know where the ball was going before the snap

Name: Hank Aaron
Fun Fact: He received death threats while chasing Babe Ruth's home run record in 1973 - and kept playing - and broke it on April 8, 1974 in front of a roaring crowd in Atlanta

Name: Jerry West
Fun Fact: His silhouette is the NBA logo - he doesn't just have his number retired, his image defines the entire sport

Name: Pedro Martinez
Fun Fact: In 1999 and 2000 he was so far ahead of his era that his statistics look like a video game - the best two-year pitching run of the modern era

Name: David Robinson
Fun Fact: He served in the US Navy for two years before joining the Spurs - The Admiral - and then drafted Tim Duncan which gave San Antonio two of the greatest PFs ever

Name: Randy Johnson
Fun Fact: The Big Unit was 6-foot-10 and threw 100mph - facing him was described by hitters as looking up at a skyscraper throwing lightning

Name: Ray Lewis
Fun Fact: He danced before every game in a way that made opposing teams genuinely afraid - the most intimidating presence in NFL history - two Super Bowl rings

Name: Brian Urlacher
Fun Fact: He was the quarterback of the Bears defense for 13 years - the fastest linebacker in the NFL who could cover wide receivers and lay out running backs

Name: Junior Seau
Fun Fact: He played 20 seasons with relentless ferocity and died in 2012 - his CTE diagnosis helped change how the NFL treats player safety

Name: Lawrence Taylor
Fun Fact: He won NFL MVP in 1986 as a defensive player - something that almost never happens - and offensive tackles literally changed how they played because of him
Notes: Widely considered the greatest defensive player in NFL history

Name: Jack Lambert
Fun Fact: He played without his two front teeth and once said quarterbacks should wear dresses because of how protected they were becoming
Notes: The face of the Steelers' Steel Curtain defense

Name: Chuck Bednarik
Fun Fact: He was the last two-way player in NFL history - played both center on offense and linebacker on defense - Concrete Charlie was a different species

Name: Mario Lemieux
Fun Fact: He survived cancer mid-career, came back and scored a goal in his first game back, and then won a second Stanley Cup - the most remarkable comeback in hockey history
Notes: #66 retired by Penguins - played on same line as Jagr #68

Name: Jaromir Jagr
Fun Fact: He chose #68 to honor the Prague Spring of 1968 when Soviet tanks rolled into Czechoslovakia - every time he put on the jersey it meant something political and personal
Notes: Adjacent to Lemieux #66 - two linemates, two Legends

Name: Evgeni Malkin
Fun Fact: He was the second-best player on his team for most of his career - which on any other team would make him the best player - three Cups with Crosby
Notes: Active but passes Legend test per tier list
Hannah is Boston wall - globally still unwritten

Name: Mean Joe Greene
Fun Fact: The Coca-Cola ad where he gives a kid his jersey made him the most famous defensive player in America - four Super Bowls and the face of the Steel Curtain

Name: Ray Bourque
Fun Fact: He gave up his #7 so Phil Esposito could retire it - wore #77 for the rest of his career - and the whole league cheered when he finally won the Cup in Colorado at 40

Notes: Bruce Smith 200 sacks - consider for v1.5

Name: Jerry Rice
Fun Fact: NFL.com named him the greatest player in NFL history - not the greatest receiver - the greatest player, period - and it's hard to argue

Name: Terrell Owens
Fun Fact: He did situps in his driveway during a public holdout and made it appointment television - one of the most explosive and controversial players in NFL history

Notes: Welker is Boston wall - globally unwritten

Name: Randy Moss
Fun Fact: He ran past defenders so easily in his prime that it looked like they were standing still - the most physically gifted receiver in NFL history
Notes: Note: wore #84 in Minnesota, #81 in New England

Name: Sidney Crosby
Fun Fact: He shot pucks at a dryer in his basement as a kid until the dryer broke - the work ethic behind three Stanley Cups and three Hart Trophies

Name: Travis Kelce
Fun Fact: He has four Super Bowl rings and is the most reliable weapon any quarterback has ever had - the greatest tight end of his era and maybe ever
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually - may move to Legend on retirement

Name: Eric Lindros
Fun Fact: He was the most physically dominant player in the NHL in the mid-90s - 6-foot-4 and could skate like a wing - concussions cut short what could have been the greatest career ever

Name: Tony Gonzalez
Fun Fact: He has 1,325 career receptions - the most by any tight end in NFL history - and redefined what the position could be
Notes: #88 is most HOF-crowded number in NFL history per tier list

Name: Mike Ditka
Fun Fact: He was the first tight end inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame - revolutionized the position - and then coached the Bears to the greatest season in NFL history in 1985

Name: Michael Strahan
Fun Fact: He set the single-season sack record with 22.5 in 2001 - and helped the Giants beat the undefeated Patriots in Super Bowl XLII - one of the greatest upsets in NFL history

Name: Reggie White
Fun Fact: The Minister of Defense - 198 career sacks - a Hall of Famer who combined uncommon physical gifts with genuine religious conviction that he wore openly

Notes: Seymour is Boston wall - globally unwritten

Name: Pavel Bure
Fun Fact: He scored 60 goals in back-to-back seasons in 1993-94 and 1994-95 - the Russian Rocket was the most electrifying player of his era

Name: Connor McDavid
Fun Fact: He skates faster than anyone who has ever played the game - multiple Hart Trophies and Art Ross Trophies - the best player in hockey right now without debate

Name: Wayne Gretzky
Fun Fact: If you remove every goal he ever scored he would still be the all-time points leader just from his assists - #99 was retired across the entire NHL in 1999
